coredata.dc:title: NIR-responsive L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs for rapid photo-triggered sterilization of multidrug-resistant uropathogens with molecular docking analysis

coredata.dc:description:
The rise of antibiotic resistance necessitates innovative and antibiotic-free bactericidal strategies. Herein, we synthesize linear polyethyleneimine- and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots-conjugated nonstoichiometric silver sulfide nanoparticles (L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs) via a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis approach for rapid photo-triggered sterilization of multidrug-resistantMDR pathogens. The NIR-light-enhanced antibacterial activity was evaluated against MDR Escherichia coli
ATCC-25922 and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
MRSA-1369, demonstrating 5- and 10-fold higher efficacy than their Ag2S and Ag counterparts, with an efficiency comparable to standard antibioticciprofloxacin. The prime antibacterial mechanism involves L-PEI-wrapping enhances electrostatic interaction, anchoring Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs to the bacterial surface, and inducing outer membrane disruption via high local heat and elevated level of reactive oxygen speciesROS—•O2 –, •OH generation under single NIR-light-irradiation, thereby achieving > 99 % bacterial eradication at a respective concentration of as low as 60 μg mL−1. Moreover, the in-silico simulations against β-lactamase
E. coli and DNA-Gyrase
MRSA aligned well with the in vitro antibacterial findings, confirming their efficacy in disrupting key bacterial intracellular functions. In short, this study develops efficient and biocompatible nonstoichiometric hybrid-NPs as superior synthetic bactericides, presenting a promising alternative to conventional antibiotics for effective antibacterial control applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram depicting the synthesis process of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid NPs.

Structures of the Ag, Ag2S, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs: (a) XRD spectra, (b) FT-IR patterns, TEM micro-images of the (c) Ag NPs, (d) Ag2S NPs, and (e) L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs, (f) HAADF-STEM image of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs, and its respective (g-j) EDX elemental mapping micro-images.

Optical and photo-chemical analysis of the Ag NPs, Ag2S NPs, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs: (a) UV–vis–NIR DRS spectra, (b) optical bandgap Tauc plot, (c) time-dependent superoxide •O2 – radicals scavenging activity both under dark and UV-Vis-NIR light irradiation conditions, (d) chemical transformation of the p-Nitroblue tetrazolium chlorideNBT to formazan, and (e) fluorescence alterations in the ROS sensor containing L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs and 3-CCA under UV-Vis-NIR light illumination.

Photothermal effects of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs (a) UV–visible absorption spectra of supernatant solution exceeding 808 nm of wavelength, (b) temperature evaluation curves with varying concentrations of NPs under NIR-laser at 2 W cm−2 power density, (c) IR thermographic images with different concentrations of the NPs treated with 808 nm NIR-laser, (d) temperature evaluation of the NPs (60 μg mL−1) over 10 on/off NIR-laser irradiation cycles, (e) heating and cooling curve of the NPs (60 μg mL−1) under 2 W cm−2 power density of NIR-laser, and (f) the cooling duration versus the negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature.

Antibacterial activity of the NPs and control groups against (a) E. coli and (b) MRSA bacterial strains with and without NIR-laser illumination treatment conditions. Bar diagrams illustrating the corresponding bacterial viability percentage of (c) E. coli and (d) MRSA after being treated with NPs and control groups at varying concentrations of 0 μg mL−1 (L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs), 60 μg mL−1 (L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs), 90 μg mL−1 (Ag2S NPs), 120 μg mL−1 (Ag NPs), and 30 μg mL−1 (ciprofloxacin), respectively. All untreated and standard antibiotic-ciprofloxacin-treated samples were employed as blank and positive control groups for the experiments, (p * < 0.05, p * *< 0.01 and p * **< 0.001, n = 3).

(a) SEM microimages of E. coli and MRSA after being treated with Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs, respectively, under dark and NIR-laser irradiation, (b) Fluorescence images of live/dead E. coli and MRSA bacteria treated with L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs with and without NIR-laser irradiation (Bar = 50 μm). The assay without (0 μg mL−1) antibacterial compounds is termed as a blank control group of the experiment.

(a) Bacterial biofilm eradication effects of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs with NIR-laser treatment against (a) E. coli and (b) MRSA bacteria, the corresponding measured OD590 to assess the presence of biofilm biomass following different treatments against (c) E. coli and (d) MRSA bacterial strains, (p * < 0.05 and p * **< 0.001, n = 3).
Schematic depiction of the possible charge transfer mechanism with proposed reactions in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs and antibacterial mechanism under NIR-laser irradiation.

The corresponding bar graphs of (a) protein levels and (b) CatalasCAT activity against both pathogenic strains. Primarily compared with each bacterial strain at the same MICs of each antibacterial NPs sample (contained a 0 μg mL−1 blank and 30 μg mL−1 ciprofloxacin-positive control samples), and then intercompared with the blank control and positive control groups at different concentrations, (p * < 0.05, p * *< 0.01 and p * **< 0.001, n = 3).

L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs superimposed docked complexes with: (a) β-lactamase
E. coli and (b) DNA-gyrase
MRSA enzymes.

(a) Digital photographs from hemolysis test of PBS (-Ve control), L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs at different concentrations (30120 μg mL−1), and Triton (+Ve control) (n = 3), (b) hemolysis ratio of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs at varying concentrations (30120 μg), p * < 0.05 p * *< 0.01, n = 3, NS means not significant.

Cytotoxicity evaluation (a) cell viability percent and (b) fluorescence images of MC3T3-C1 cells cultured with different concentrations of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs sample for one-day incubation (scale bar = 20 μm, p * < 0.05, p * *< 0.01 and p * **< 0.001, n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The rise of antibiotic resistance necessitates innovative and antibiotic-free bactericidal strategies. Herein, we synthesize linear polyethyleneimine- and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots-conjugated nonstoichiometric silver sulfide nanoparticles (L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs) via a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis approach for rapid photo-triggered sterilization of multidrug-resistantMDR pathogens. The NIR-light-enhanced antibacterial activity was evaluated against MDR Escherichia coli
ATCC-25922 and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
MRSA-1369, demonstrating 5- and 10-fold higher efficacy than their Ag2S and Ag counterparts, with an efficiency comparable to standard antibioticciprofloxacin. The prime antibacterial mechanism involves L-PEI-wrapping enhances electrostatic interaction, anchoring Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs to the bacterial surface, and inducing outer membrane disruption via high local heat and elevated level of reactive oxygen speciesROS—•O2 –, •OH generation under single NIR-light-irradiation, thereby achieving > 99 % bacterial eradication at a respective concentration of as low as 60 μg mL−1. Moreover, the in-silico simulations against β-lactamase
E. coli and DNA-Gyrase
MRSA aligned well with the in vitro antibacterial findings, confirming their efficacy in disrupting key bacterial intracellular functions. In short, this study develops efficient and biocompatible nonstoichiometric hybrid-NPs as superior synthetic bactericides, presenting a promising alternative to conventional antibiotics for effective antibacterial control applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Antibiotics have revolutionized medicine, saving countless lives annually. However, the misuse of conventional antibiotics or the inadequate development of new antibiotics has fueled the emergence of antibiotic-resistant pathogens. This rise in antibiotic resistance is a leading cause of nosocomial infections with Acinetobacter baumannii, Enterococcus faecalis, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
MRSA, and Enterobacter species., being the most frequently isolated uropathogens[1–3]. These pathogens are resilient communities of microorganisms enclosed within a self-produced extracellular matrix, enabling them to attach to various biotic and abiotic surfaces, and are responsible for a wide variety of infections[2,4]. For instance, only a single MRSA bacterium can cause localized purulent infections, sepsis, pneumonia, and other systematic infections, with an estimated latent risk of causing more than 10 million annual deaths[5]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop safe, effective, and antibiotic-free strategies with innovative mechanisms of action to combat antibiotic resistance and disinfect the environment.

In past decades, light-activated sterilization techniques, such as photodynamic antibacterialPDA and photothermal antibacterialPTA approaches have emerged as effective and safe strategies to combat multidrug-resistantMDR microbes[6,7]. These techniques utilize photoresponsive materials to generate reactive oxygen speciesROS, induce hyperthermia, or combine both effects under specific light wavelengths, resulting in bacterial cell apoptosis[8]. Nonetheless, both PDA and PTA therapies have some kind of limitations, with PDA therapy restricted by shallow tissue penetration and potential phototoxicity, while PTA therapy may struggle with precise heat control and tissue damage[6,7]. Significant efforts have recently been devoted to the development of photoresponsive platforms that combine photodynamic and photothermal modalities to enhance microbial eradication. These platforms use specific light wavelengths to simultaneously generate ROS and hyperthermia, while integrating functional materials to improve their targeting specificity and minimize off-target effects, ensuring effective bactericidal activity[9]. Numerous studies have previously highlighted the encouraging combination of PDA and PTA therapies to augment the photoresponsive therapeutic efficacy and mitigate the associated limitations, such as Ag2S@ZIF-90/Arg[10], AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti[11], Ag2S/V2CTx@PDA[12], Zn-doped g-C3N4/Bi2S3 [13], Protease-loaded CuS[14], BSA@CuS@PpIX[15], and BiOI@Bi2S3/MXene[16], composite materials. However, despite their significant potential, these materials often encounter complex preparation processes and absorption mismatches between their photothermal and photodynamic components, which can reduce the efficacy of simultaneous phototherapy, thereby complicating treatment protocols and prolonging therapy duration[9,17,18]. Therefore, it is important to develop a desired combined bactericidal platform that integrates “all-in-one” nanocomposite with facile fabrication, enhanced ROS generation, and excellent photothermal conversion efficiency, all effectively triggered by a single wavelength of light for advanced antibacterial applications.

Recent decades witnessed the rapid development of synergistic PDA and PTA nanoplatforms, among which inorganic materials, such as metals-based nanomaterialsAu, Ag, Cu[19–21], metal oxide nanocompositesCuO, ZnO, TiO2 [22], carbon-based nano-hybridsGO, g-C3N4, CNTs[20], and two-dimensional hybrid nanosheetsMoS2, WS2, TiS2 [23] have gained eminence attentions in phototriggered bacterial eradication due to their exceptional stability, efficacy, and superior ROS generation or photothermal conversion potentials. Among these, silver nanoparticlesAg NPs are potent broad-spectrum bactericidal agents due to the detachment of silver ionsAg+, which interact and disrupt cell membranes and enzymes[24–26]. However, the high cost, susceptibility to agglomeration, and excessive Ag+ leakage, which can cause potential toxicity and lead to conditions like argyrosis, spasms, and gastrointestinal disorders, thus greatly hampering their applications in biological therapies[24,26]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a precise Ag-based nano- system with both high antibacterial efficacy and controlled Ag+ release to address these associated challenges. Encouragingly, silver sulfideAg2S is a binary compound that offers several advantages of reduced acute toxicity, acceptable biocompatibility, high stability against metal leakage, and multiple bacterial killing mechanisms[23,27]. Importantly, its strong NIR absorbance, remarkable optical-to-thermal conversion efficiency, significant X-ray attenuation coefficient, and impressive photodynamic characteristics all of which contribute to its growing feasibility for potential future applications[28]. Despite these advantages, the antibacterial effectiveness of pristine Ag2S NPs is restricted by several important factors, such as its low bandgap (⁓0.9–1.2 eV) and strong interatomic bonding leads to rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriersPCCs, consequently suppressing antibacterial ROS generation and photothermal effects[28,29]. Additionally, Ag2S can easily agglomerate into bulk NPs during synthesis, leading to an inert nature that hinders its interaction with microbes and results in poor antibacterial efficacy[23,28]. To address these limitations, strategies like synthesizing ultrasmall nanoarchitectures with suitable electronic band alignments, chemical doping/coating, and hybridizing with various carbon-based materials (e.g., carbon quantum dots, nanotubular carbon structures, graphene derivatives, MXenes, etc.,) have been explored to enhance the antibacterial potential of pristine metal sulfides[30–33]. According to Prakash et al., Ag2S NPs of about 44 nm in size demonstrated more effective antimicrobial activity than the larger Ag2S NPs due to the quantum confinement effect and enhanced charge carrier transport[30]. Similarly, surface modification through doping/coating can significantly enhance the mobility of charge carriers and promote electrostatic interaction with the bacterial cell membrane, facilitating material penetration into the lipid bilayer[28,32,34]. Moloto et al., reported the use of chitosan, combretum molle, and black wattle-capped Ag2S NPs for effective eradication of MDR pathogens with efficient PCCs separation, contributing to enhanced antimicrobial activity[35]. Additionally, developing heterostructures from multiple functional materials with aligned electronic bands can promote electron/holese-/h+ mobility and materials stability, thereby boosting their photocatalytic antibacterial performance[23,36]. Asma et al., reported heteroatoms (N, S)-doped carbon-modified TiS2 nanohybrids exhibited enhanced photodriven sterilization efficiency against both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Escherichia coli bacterial strains due to their efficient charge carrier separation, multi-mechanistic cytotoxicity, and ability to induce sever damage to pathogenic cells[32]. Another study by the same group revealed that, polydopamine-coated SnS/g-C3N4 nanocomposites demonstrated excellent antibacterial performance due to the efficient ROS generation, enabled by enhanced charge carrier separation and transport, thereby leading to effective bacterial membrane disruption[34]. Through further investigations, it has been elucidated that biocompatible polymer coatings on inorganic nanomaterials improve cytocompatibility and significantly enhance the heterogeneous interfaces of the materials, while incorporating doping strategies and heterostructures design further optimizes their antibacterial properties[23,37,38].

Accordingly, engineering an Ag2S matrix with sulfurS-x vacancies by incorporating it with S-doped carbon quantum dotsS-Cdots results in a hybrid nanostructure with potentially enhanced performance. The introduction of S-x-vacancies creates additional active sites within the Ag2S matrix for more efficient charge transfer, while S-Cdots enhance photoluminance and electron mobility, which collectively enable effective bacterial eradication through combined photodynamic and photothermal effects. Furthermore, polyethyleneiminePEI is a weakly alkaline polymer with high-density amine groups, exhibiting a “proton sponge effect” that facilitates its interaction with biological systems by allowing protonation at various pH levels[39,40]. Typically, the cationic nature of linear PEI (L-PEI) promotes electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged surfaces of nonstoichiometric Ag2S-based hybrid-NPs, enhancing their absorption and stability in both aqueous and biological media[39–41], while offering additional advantages over branched PEI (B-PEI), including easy synthesis, higher yields, better conductivity, cost-effective monomers repeat unit of (C2H5N)n, and notably superior biocompatibility[42–44]. Pandey et al., reported that L-PEI/DNA complexes demonstrate enhanced cell viability and nuclear localizations than B-PEI/DNA complexes due to its reduced cytotoxicity and lowered molecular weightMw [45]. The electrostatic interactions between the L-PEI ammoniumNH4 + groups and the nucleic acids phosphatePO4
groups enable L-PEI attachment to microbial cell walls, allowing dopantions to react with cellular components and induce targeted cells lysis, which ultimately highlights its strong antibacterial activity[46,47]. Extensive research findings have highlighted that lower Mw L-PEI-functionalized metal sulfides and their composites demonstrate superior biocidal performance compared to its higher Mw L-PEI or B-PEI counterparts[48–50]. The polymer-modified Ag2S matrix with S-x-vacancies and S-Cdots not only yields photo-triggered bioactive nanoagents with improved conductivity, while also provides a viable solution in addressing Ag+ leaching and Ag2S agglomeration. Despite extensive research on the antibacterial functionalities of hybrid nanomaterials, there remains a scarcity of studies evaluating their efficacy against clinically isolated drug-resistant pathogens. Addressing this research gap, our study systematically investigates the synergistic effects of S-Cdots compositing- and L-PEI coating on enhancing the bactericidal potential of nonstoichiometric Ag2S-x NPs. This tailored nanohybrid not only facilitates controlled ROS generation and photothermal conversion efficiency under NIR irradiation but also ensures enhanced bacterial targeting and biofilm disruption. These findings provide mechanistic insights into the rational design of metal sulfide-based antibacterial agents, distinguishing this work from previous studies that lack a comprehensive evaluation of drug-resistant pathogenic resistance mechanisms.

Herein, we present the aqueous synthesis of cytocompatible cationic nonstoichiometric Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs wrapped with L-PEI using a one-step, cost-effective solvothermal synthesis technique to achieve highly efficient antibacterial agents ( Fig. 1). The resultant hybrid-NPs demonstrate strong photodriven bactericidal effects on both tested Gram-negative–Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC-25922) and Gram-positive–methicilline-resistant Staphylococus aureus (MRSA-1369) bacterial strains at the lowest designated minimum inhibitory concentrationMIC of 60 μg mL−1, respectively, which is comparable to the performance of the standard antibiotic-ciprofloxacin. The heightened antibacterial effect of the resulting hybrid-NPs is attributed to their synergistic effect, with the L-PEI coating augmenting the electrostatic interaction, anchoring Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs to the bacterial cell wall, leading to outer membrane disruption through localized heating and increased amounts of ROS generation, as well as controlled release of Ag+ under a single wavelength (808 nm) of NIR-laser irradiation. Furthermore, the in-silico molecular docking studies were conducted to assess the binding affinity of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs with β-lactamase and DNA-Gyrase proteins obtained from E. coli and MRSA strains, demonstrating their efficacy to disrupt critical bacterial intracellular processes. These findings highlight the potential of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs for developing innovative antiseptic materials, capable of effectively disinfecting various MDR pathogens to reduce the high risk associated with conventional antibiotics abuse. To sum up, this study stands as a pioneering and significant contribution to the field of synthetic bactericides, focusing on investigating the NIR-responsive multimodal in vitro antibacterial activity and conducting in-silico molecular docking studies of the nonstoichiometric L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥ 99.0 wt%), sodium sulfide (Na2S, ≥ 99.0 wt%), L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, ≥ 98.0 wt%), Oleylamine (OAm, ≥ 68.0 wt%), ethylene glycol (EG, ≥ 99.0 wt%), linear polyethyleneimine (L-PEI, Mn= 25 kDa), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH ∼7.4), hexane (C6H14, ≥ 99.9 wt%), and anhydrous ethanol (EtOH) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. The in vitro case-control study was conducted at the School of Microbiology, South China University Technology, China. The antibacterial activity of the prepared nanomaterials was examined against E. coli
ATCC-25922, as a standard reference strain, and 12 clinically isolated MDR E. coli strains, which were confirmed to be MDR according to the criteria outlined in previous references[51,52]. Additionally, the study also utilized MRSA-1369, a clinically isolated MDR strain of Staphylococcus aureus, which is known for its resistance to multiple antibiotics[53], to access the nanomaterials efficacy against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive pathogens. The isolates were sourced from a healthcare facility for further analysis. Furthermore, the nutrient Agar and nutrient broth were procured from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Texas, USA. For bacterial growth screening and materials bactericidal evaluation, Muller-Hinton AgarMHA and nutrient Luria BrothLB were respectively used as the bacterial culture media. Bactericidal activity in terms of minimum inhibitory concentrationsMICs was performed utilizing 96-well microtiter plates, and a photometer reader for precise measurement. The experiments involved the use of various antibacterial chemicals and/or solvents, all of which were of analytical grade and utilized without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of L-PEI conjugated Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs were successfully synthesized using an adopted version of a previously established synthesis method, as illustrated in Fig. 1 [33]. In a typical preparation method, 3 mM of AgNO3 was dissolved in 20 mL of OleylamineOAm at 24 ± 2 °C of room temperatureRT. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of L-PEI (0.15 g mL−1) and 0.25 g of C6H8O6 were first dissolved in 10 mL PBS buffer (7 mM) and then added to the above-running solution and kept under vigorous stirring until the uniform suspension was formed and marked it as solution-A. Similarly, 3 mM of Na2S was completely dissolved in 20 mL of Ethylene GlycolEG and was labeled as solution-B. Both solutions (at a 1:1 ratio) were carefully mixed and continuously stirred with a magnetic stirrer to ensure their complete dissolution. Then, 40 mL of the total mixture was placed into a 50 mL high-pressure stainless steel autoclave reactor lined with quartz. The reactor was degassed with the nitrogenN2 flow for 30 min at 24 ± 2 °C to prevent the formation of secondary phases. After degassing with N2 gas, the reaction mixture underwent sequential heat treatments; first at 60 °C for 1 h, followed by an increase to 180 °C for 4 h at 1.5 °C min−1 ramp rate. After the reaction temperature naturally returned to RT, the solid precipitates were separated via centrifugation, and washed multiple times using a mixed solvent of absolute ethanol and hexane (at a 2:1 ratio), followed by rinsing with deionized water. Finally, the samples were vacuum-dried at 70 °C for 12 h. Notably, the yield of the resultant sample was determined after the final drying step, consistently achieving high values of approximately 450 mg due to the controlled and optimized one-step synthesis process. For comparison, Ag NPs and Ag2S NPs were prepared using the same synthesis protocol, with the only difference of without the addition of sulfur source (Na2S) and L-PEI/C6H8O6 reagents in the case of Ag and Ag2S NPs, respectively.

Materials phase identification was confirmed using an X-ray diffractometerXRD, employing a JDX–3532 model by JEOL─Japan. The instrument operated in the 2θ range of 10°–80° with Cu–Kα radiations (wavelength: 0.1540 nm). Molecular composition and surface functional groups were confirmed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometerFTIR, Nexus-870, optimized in the wavenumbers ranging from 380 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer equipped with a He-Ne laser at a wavelength of 530 nm. Morphological characterization and surface-coating investigation were conducted using transmission electron microscopyTEM with a JEOL JEM–2100 model. High-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopyHAADF-STEM micrograph and its corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopyEDX elemental mapping were obtained using the nanoprobe-TEM mode FEI-Talos microscope, operated at 200 KV accelerating voltage with a camera length of nearly 400 mm. An elemental composition study of the resultant material was also conducted using energy dispersive spectroscopyEDS attached to a JSM-7500F electron microscope by JEOL-Japan. Optical absorption and diffuse reflectance properties, along with energy bandgaps were determined using a Shimadzue UV-3600 Plus UVVisibleNIR spectrometer, covered 185–3400 nm spectral range. RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) operating with a wide spectral range of Ex. ∼200–900 nm/Em. ∼220–900 nm attached with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 400 V was used to record the photoluminescencePL spectra. The photoelectrochemicalPEC measurements were carried out by Epsiloneclipse electrochemical workstation with three home-made electrode quartz cells. To detect the ROS radicals, a Magnetech ESR5000–Bruker, Germany model of electron spin resonanceESR spectrometer was employed and all ESR measurements were conducted at RT. The ROS scavenging experiments under UV-NIR light irradiation were conducted using a 66483-Xe & Hg(Xe) high-pressure light source with a power output capacity of 200–500 W housing lamp. The evaluation of bacterial protein levels and catalaseCAT activity was accomplished via an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assayELISA on a clinical laboratory instrument. The enzyme labeling and measurements were performed through a Varioskan LUX multi-mode microplate reader by Fisher Scientific.

To assess the photothermal response of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs at varying concentrations (0, 15, 30, and 60 μg mL−1) dissolved in water solution were subjected to NIR-laser irradiation (wavelength: 808 nm, power density: 2.0 W cm−2, and spot size: 1.5 cm2) for 8 min. Both the temperature variation and thermographs were then recorded at 30 s intervals using an infrared thermal imaging camera (FLIR, A350, USA). Additionally, the photothermal stability test was also conducted by illuminating the NPs with NIR-laser for consecutive 10 on-off cycles. The corresponding photothermal conversion efficiency of the resultant NPs was determined using the previously reported protocol[54,55]. Typically, 1 mL suspension of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs with varying concentrations ranging from 0 to 60 μg mL−1 was illuminated at 2 W cm−2 for 8 min of 808 nm NIR-light. After reaching a steady-state temperature, the excitation light was turned off, allowing the temperature to return to its initial state, and the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated using the following Eq. 1.

To evaluate the antibacterial effectiveness of the well-synthesized hybrid-NPs, two bacterial strains, E. coli (ATCC-25922) and MRSA-1369 were selected as the targeted pathogens. Before the start of the antibacterial activity test, the required glassware experimental stuff were sterilized at 110 °C for 30 min in an open-air microwave oven with an additional 30 min UV-light irradiation to avoid the latent risk of environmental contamination. Both pathogenic bacteria inoculums were prepared in LB nutrient broth using a direct colony suspension method. Each bacterial suspension was adjusted to a density of 5 × 105 to 5 × 106 CFU mL−1 via 0.5 McFarland standard and properly diluted by transferring 0.2 mL of each bacterial aliquot to 1.8 mL of sterile LB broth saline solution to achieve a mid-exponential growth phase, resulting in a harvested density concentration of 5 × 106 to 5 × 107 CFU mL−1. The bacterial suspension was subjected to multiple washes using a sterilized saline solutionPBS containing 0.75 % NaCl, followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min to isolate the bacterial cells. Subsequently, the bacterial cells were resuspended in PBS solution and 0.2 mL of each strain sample was aseptically inoculated into 0.8 mL of already prepared antibacterial stock solutions containing varying concentrations (0, 60, 90, and 120 μg mL−1) of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots, Ag2S, and Ag NPs. Standard antibioticciprofloxacin was utilized as a positive control at a concentration of 30 μg mL−1 to evaluate the antibacterial performance of the tested samples under comparable conditions. Next, the subsequent mixed suspensions (except ciprofloxacin) were irradiated with and without NIR-laser for 8 min. Finally, 0.2 mL of each sample mixture was inoculated into the MHA plates using the pour plates technique with gentle swirling to ensure the even distribution of the sample. The resulting bacterial cultured plates were then systematically incubated at 37 ℃ temperature for 16 h (in case of E. coli) and 20 h (in case of MRSA), after which the number of bacterial colonies was counted for all treated samples. All reference samples without antibacterial compounds and NIR-light illumination were marked as blank and negative (-Ve) control groups of the experiments. The percentage rate of bacterial survival was then calculated according to Eq. 4, where N0 represents the counts of colony forming unitsCFUs in the blank group while N denotes the counts of CFUs in the treated group, respectively.

The MICs of the tested materials against two bacterial strains were evaluated using a microbroth dilution technique on 96-well microtiter plates, followed a previously reported protocol[56]. A two-fold serial dilution was performed using a stock solution of 2 mg mL−1, resulting in concentrations ranging from 2 to 0.0643 mg mL−1. Bacterial suspensions were standardized to match the 2.0 McFarland standard, resulting in a final concentration of 5 × 106 to 5 × 107 CFU mL−1. Subsequently, 0.2 mL of LB containing bacterial strains and varying amounts (0, 60, 90, and 120 μg mL−1) of the antibacterial compounds were added to the topmost rows of the microtiter plates. Next, 0.2 mL of liquid LB containing 30 μg mL−1 ciprofloxacin and each bacterial strain were serially filled the wells of the next rows. For reference, blank LB-broth was filled to the wells of the subsequent row. Similarly, LB containing only antibacterial compounds and/or bacterial strains were sequentially pipetted into the wells of the next rows. The plates were then incubated aerobically at 37 °C for 18 h. The blank control groups contained LB with bacterial strains but without antibacterial compounds, the negative control groups comprised LB with antibacterial compounds but without bacterial strains, and the positive control groups contained ciprofloxacin and bacterial strains. After incubation, the MIC was determined as the lowest dilution of the antibacterial compounds which resulted in a noticeable reduction in bacterial growth within the LB-broth.

To enumerate the generation of ROS in the synthesized materials, we employed 2, 7 dichlorohydihydrofluorescein diacetateDCFH-DA, comarine-3-carboxylic acid3-CCA, and p-Nitroblu tetrazolium chloridep-NBT as prob molecules in the detection of active species. By measuring the fluorescence signals of the ROS sensor with an excitation/emission wavelength (Ex. 488 nm/Em. 525 nm), we assessed the hydroxyl (•OH) radicals’ generation in subsequent samples, including 2 mM 3-CCA treated with and without UV–vis–NIR light illumination, L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs treated with UV–vis–NIR light illumination, and ROS sensor containing L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs treated with UV–vis–NIR light illumination. In addition, we also examined the time-dependent ROS concentration in the resultant materials by transforming p-NBT to formazan in the existence of superoxide •O2 – radicals as a ROS sensor. The fluorescence intensities of all treated samples were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (Model: EDINBURG, FS5).

The in silico molecular docking study of the as-prepared hybrid-NPs was conducted against two different β-lactamase and DNA-gyrase enzymes in E. coli and MRSA, respectively. The selected proteins are essential for bacterial cell wall synthesis and nucleic acid replication, making them key targets. Both β-lactamase
E. coli , (PDB 4KZ9, resolution 1.72 Ǻ) and DNA-gyrase MRSA (PDB 5MMN. Resolution 1.90 Ǻ) crystal structures were acquired from the protein data bank[32]. The protein structures were processed using a molecular operating environmentMOE preparation module, subjected to 3D protonation for stability, and then energy minimized. Protonation and energy minimization of all compounds was performed using MOE’s default parameters, including a gradient of 0.05 and MMFF94X force field. For molecular docking, MOE’s default settings were applied: Triangle Matcher for Placement, London dG for Rescoring,-1, Forcefield for Refinement, and GBVI/WSA for Rescoring-2. To obtain multiple conformations for each ligand, ten conformations were generated and the top-ranked based on docking scores were selected for analyzing protein-ligand interactions. Both interaction and visualization were conducted using PyMOL software[32,33].

To confirm the reliability of the experimental results, the obtained data were expressed as the Means ± SD (means ± standard deviation), n = 3. The obtained p-values less than 0.05 (denoted as p * < 0.05, p * * < 0.01, and p * ** < 0.001) represent statistical significance, reflecting a high degree of confidence in the experimental findings. The statistical analysis was conducted using a t-test in Origin 2018 software. The biocidal effectiveness of the prepared NPs was comprehensively assessed at various concentrations and compared with both the blank and +Ve/−Ve control groups of the experiment.

Fig. 2a shows the typical XRD patterns of the well-grown Ag, Ag2S, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs, respectively. The pattern clearly illustrates the main peaks appeared at 2θ of 38.9 º, 44.37 º, 64.56º, and 77.46º correspond precisely to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystallographic planes of the face-centered cubicfcc structure of the Ag (JCPDS #04–0783)[57]. In contrast, the diffraction spectrum of the Ag2S NPs exhibits plentiful well-known peaks positioned at (2θ) 26.25º, 28.89º, 31.49º, 33.89º, 34.50º, 36.78º, 37.80º, 40.74º, 43.50º, 44.76º, 45.47º, 46.10º, 47.69º, 53.32º, 58.19º, 63.83º, 69.97º, and 72.21º correspond to crystallographic planes of (−101), (111), (−112), (120), (−121), (121),(−103), (031), (200), (103), (023), (113), (311), (22−2), (042), (034), (301), and (−321), respectively, aligning with the monoclinic phase of the Ag2S (JCPDS #014–0072)[58]. Similar diffraction peaks were observed for the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample except a broad peak appeared at 2θ of 23.71º attributed to the carbon dots[59], accompanied by a noticeable reduction in overall diffraction intensities compared to the Ag2S NPs. This decrease in intensities can be explained by the amorphous nature of the L-PEI, which lacks a distant crystalline structure, leading to diminished diffraction intensity in contrast to crystalline nanomaterials. Consequently, the existence of L-PEI on the Ag2S-x/S-Cdots surface impedes X-ray diffraction, resulting in reduced peak intensity in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample. This reduction in peak intensity has been commonly observed in numerous materials when interacting with polymeric coating materials[33,34,41]. To further validate the formation of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs, the FTIR study was performed to investigate the presence of distinct surface functional groups in the samples. The spectra depicted in Fig. 2b reveal discernable vibrational patterns corresponding to the Ag, Ag2S, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs, respectively. The fundamental vibrational bands observed between 500 and 650 cm−1 of the wavenumbers range are notably associated with the specific chemical bond formation, suggesting their characteristic assignments. The FTIR spectrum of the Ag NPs reveals distinct peaks corresponding to various characteristic bands, including C-H (534 cm−1, 637 cm−1, 2853 cm−1, 2925 cm−1) stretching of aldehydes, CO (1655 cm−1) stretching of tertiary amides, C-O (1245 cm−1) stretching of benzine ring compound, C-N (1386 cm−1, 1097 cm−1) stretching vibration of aromatic and aliphatic amines, N-H (1530 cm−1) bending of secondary amines, and O-H (3330 cm−1) stretching vibration of surface adsorbed water molecules (like organic acid)[57,60]. In contrast, the FTIR spectrum of the Ag2S NPs features distinctive peaks, in which the fundamental peaks are positioned at 534 cm–1, 641 cm−1, and 2190 cm−1 assigned to the Ag–S stretching mode, indicating the presence of characteristic vibrational bands associated with the metal sulfide in the system[35]. The three additional peaks observed at 1165 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, and 2190 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration mode of the C-N, C-C (aromatic ring), and Ag-S, respectively[35,58]. Notably, a slight shift in the CO stretching vibration band (1655 cm−1) towards higher wavenumbers and corresponding broadening in the O-H vibrational band (3330 cm−1) are observed for the Ag2S NPs sample, indicating structural change and adsorption characteristics. In the case of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs, the FTIR spectrum demonstrates a noticeable alteration in the respective spectrum, highlighting the impact of incorporating L-PEI and S-Cdots onto Ag2S NPs. A notable change observed is the merging and slight shifting of the vibrational bands located at 1097 cm−1 and 1165 cm−1 into a broader band centered at 1145 cm−1. This shift implies potential intermolecular interactions among the Ag2S, S-Cdots, and the cationic nature of the L-PEI, signifying a combined structural and chemical influence of the L-PEI and S-Cdots on the Ag2S milieu. Further, the absorption peak associated with Ag-S stretching vibration band at 2190 cm−1 has slightly shifted to a lower frequency range with sharper peak in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs suggesting the formation of S-x-vacancies in the Ag2S matrix. These vacancies likely cause distortions in the Ag-S bonds and modify the electronic environment, resulting in the observed shifts and peak sharpening. In addition, an apparent shift in the CO (1655 cm−1) and O-H (3330 cm−1) stretching vibration bands to lower wavenumbers, together with a noticeable peak sharpening signify structural variation and adsorption characteristics, resulting from the synergistic effects of the L-PEI coating and S-Cdots incorporation, collectively enhance hydrogen bonding interactions and contribute to the overall stability of the nanocomposite. This alteration arises from the structural alignment of the nanostructured molecular chains in the polymeric materials[33,61], and the contributions of both the S-x-vacancies and S-Cdots, ultimately facilitating the formation of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs. Additionally, the distinctive spectrum of the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample suggests a structured arrangement and aligned conformation of all constituents in the system, which aligns well with the obtained XRD findings.

The structural morphology and chemical composition of the as-prepared samples were examined through TEM, and HAADF-STEM characterizations, along with the corresponding EDX elemental mapping images, as shown in Fig. 2(c-i). The TEM image of the Ag sample in Fig. 2c shows the presence of small spherical shape NPs with distinct morphology. Conversely, the Ag2S sample displays agglomerated NPs, resulting in an enlarged particle size compared to its Ag counterpart (Fig. 2d). The introduction of the sulfur content to the Ag2S NPs system leads to a considerable increase in the particle size due to the aggregation phenomenon. A similar effect of particle aggregation was also observed in the previously reported work of literatures[62]. During the sulfurization process, a transformative phenomenon is observed wherein small bridges form between the Ag NPs. This process leads to obscured visibility of the initial NPs, ultimately resulting in the creation of somehow amorphous and irregularly shaped nanospheres. Furthermore, the L-PEI- and S-Cdots-modified Ag2S sample in Fig. 2e revealed a notably smaller particle size in comparison to the unmodified Ag2S NPs. This reduction in particle size can be attributed to the abundant amine groups in L-PEI, which contributes to its high reactivity, solubility, and ability to chelate with transition metal sulfides, while the incorporation of S-Cdots further helps in stabilizing the nanostructure, preventing agglomeration and promoting a more uniform distribution of the hybrid-NPs. This discernible visual distinction arises from the color contrast, with the L-PEI layer appearing lighter, while the Ag2S NPs exhibit a darker hue, and the S-Cdots are visible as small white dots. An intricate core-shell structure is apparent in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots sample, featuring a well-adhered L-PEI layer enveloping the Ag2S NPs, while the S-Cdots are embedded within the Ag2S matrix. This distinctive arrangement highlights the successful formation of a protective and stabilizing L-PEI layer, complimented by the presence of S-Cdots, which collectively contribute to the stability and structural integrity of the resultant hybrid-NPs. Moreover, HAADF-STEM analysis (Fig. 2f) and corresponding EDX elemental composition mapping images in Fig. 2g-j confirm the uniform dispersion of Ag, S, N, and C elements in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample. Additionally, the EDS data (Figure S1) further ensures the existence of expected elemental peaks in the respective spectrum, with an inset table detailing the atomic/weight percentages of the elemental compositions. These observations strongly support the morphological characteristics and elemental distribution in the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs.

The UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra and optical energy bandgaps of the prepared NPs are presented in Fig. 3(a, b). A typical absorption spectrum for the Ag, Ag2S, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs exhibits a distinctive optical absorption band centered at approximately 447 nm, 451 nm, and 459 nm with the cumulative slope in the UV–visible to NIR region (Fig. 3a), respectively. These absorption bands correspond to the existence of metallic Ag NPs, characterized by their evident and encompassing nature, representing the

phenomenon of local surface plasmon resonanceLSPR. The LSPR is a phenomenon that primarily controls the as-generated absorption spectra, confirming the presence of metal NPs in the samples[63]. According to Mie’s theory[64], the absorption spectrum displayed a single LSPR band indicating the presence of spherical-shaped NPs, leading to a decrease in NPs symmetry as the number of LSPR bands increases. The reaction mixture of the resultant sample in this study demonstrates three main LSPR bands located at 592, 718, and 863 nm revealing its spherically shaped structure with a reduction in the particle size compared to its Ag2S NPs counterpart. This assignment is consistent with the obtained TEM results. Additionally, the strong absorption in the UV–visible zone suggests the NPs potential for effective visible-light harvesting, while the up and extended absorption tail into the visible-NIR region is attributed to the generation of S-x-vacancies in the system due to the presence of S-Cdots. These features enhance charge carriers’ separation and increase the concentration of holes in the valence band, thereby contributing to improved photocatalytic performance in light-driven applications[33]. To further investigate the absorbance properties, the bandgap energies were determined by drawing the linear relationship between the optical absorption edge (αhν)n, and the photon energy axis (hν), as depicted in Fig. 3b. Here, n denotes the nature of the bandgap with n = 2 for a direct bandgap and n = ½ for an indirect bandgap, hv signifies the incident photon energy, and α represents the absorption coefficient[32]. Through analyzing Fig. 3b Tauc plot, the obtained bandgap energies for the Ag NPs, Ag2S NPs, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs are respectively 2.07 eV, 2.29 eV, and 2.49 eV, indicating that the incorporation of the sulfur increases the bandgap of the Ag2S NPs compared to Ag NPs and the subsequent L-PEI coating and S-Cdots compositing further broadens the bandgap compared to Ag2S NPs sample. The broadening of the bandgap in the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs significantly reduces the electron-hole pairs recombination for enhanced photocatalytic efficiency in contrast to Ag2S NPs. This improvement is attributed to the combined effects of the L-PEI coating-induced surface passivation and the creation of S-x-vacancies by the integration of S-Cdots which collectively enhance absorption intensity and promote efficient charge carrier separation[32–34]. Consequently, the superior electron-hole transport mechanism positions L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs as a promising candidate for photodynamic bacterial inactivation activities. Prior to investigate the photo-triggered ROS-mediated bactericidal potential, additional confirmation of the charge carrier’s separation and transport mechanism in the prepared hybrid materials is necessary. To achieve this goal of the study, photoluminancePL and photoelectrochemical measurements (Figure S2a-c) were conducted to assess the reduced charge carrier’s recombination rate in the materials (see the ESI for details). Based on the optical and photoelectrochemical characteristics, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs exhibit reduced interfacial charge resistance and enhanced electrical conductivity, facilitating swift transport of photogenerated charge carriers when exposed to UV–vis–NIR light, thus highlighting their potential effectiveness in addressing bacterial inactivation challenges.

To investigate the direct presence of active species in the well-prepared hybrid-NPs, different scavenging experiments were performed to identify the principal radicals and/or holesh+ involved in the photodynamic reactions. For direct detection of the active radicals, three distinct probe-molecules, such as 2, 7 dichlorohydihydrofluorescein diacetateDCFH-DA, p-NBT, and 3-CCA were strategically employed as sacrificial agents. The involvement of these probe molecules facilitates the observation and subsequent analysis of their interaction with the active species in the system. This approach offers profound insights into the presence and dynamic behavior of these key entities, thereby enhancing our comprehension of their integral role within the intricate photocatalytic process. P-NBT was selectively employed to discern the presence of •O2 – species, while 3-CCA served as a sensitive indicator for detecting both OH• species and h+ in the respective reaction mixture of the samples. The concentration of the •O2 – radicals generated during the photo-catalytic reaction is shown in Fig. 3c. After an incubation period of 120 minutes under UV-Vis-NIR irradiation, the Ag NPs, Ag2S NPs, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs samples yielded measurable quantities of the •O2 – species, such as 53.5 µmol L−1, 67.2 µmol L−1, and 75.9 µmol L−1, respectively. Conversely, no detectable formation of •O2 – species was observed in the absence of UV-Vis-NIR irradiation within given incubation period, confirming the light-driven nature of the photocatalytic process. These findings further indicate an augmented generation of •O2 – species upon the introduction of L-PEI and S-Cdots within Ag2S in contrast to the Ag2S and Ag counterparts. The results featured in Fig. 3c were derived from a thorough investigation of the interaction between the p-NBT and •O2 – species, established at a precisely controlled ratio of 1:4. This essential association in the form of reaction is graphically represented in Fig. 3d, which effectively illustrating the reckonable measurement of the •O2 – radicals through the delicate use of the p-NBT as a perceptive probe-molecule. These consequential results offer profound insights into the generation and detection of the •O2 – radicals in the photodynamic framework, thereby amplifying our understanding of their origination, characterization, and crucial role in the system. To further substantiate the photochemical attributes of the prepared NPs, additional experiments were conducted using 3-CCA molecules as a target substrate to scavenge OH• and h+ species. These experiments were performed under precisely controlled experimental conditions, involving UV–vis–NIR radiations emitting from a high-pressure LED lamp source under ambient temperature. The direct interaction of the OH• radicals exist in the targeted material and 3-CCA molecules resulted in a remarkable generation of 7-hydroxycumarin-3-carboxylic acid, exhibiting distinct and noticeable fluorescence characteristics. The concern reaction precisely produced the intended fluorescent species, facilitating accurate observation and analysis of the fluorescence phenomenon in the targeted materials. In perfect alignment with these principles, the results presented in Fig. 3e demonstrate that upon UV–vis–NIR-irradiation of the reaction mixture containing L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs and 3-CCA, a notable and substantial generation of the 7-hydroxycomarin-3-CCA molecules with pronounced fluorescent properties has occurred. Furthermore, the distinguished augmentation in the fluorescence with the increase of applied radiation doseJ strongly indicates the existence of OH• species in the system. The heightened fluorescence is likely ascribed to the negative hydroxyl group existing in the Ag2S-x component of the resultant sample. On the other hand, there were no appreciable alterations detected in the fluorescence of the non-irradiated samples mixture, nor in the case of irradiated samples containing only L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs or 3-CCA, respectively. In addition, the direct oxidation of 3-CCA molecules can be theoretically explained through the existence of chlorine ions in the reaction mixture. These chlorine ions act as effective traps for the photogenerated h+ in the system, resulting in the observed phenomenon of direct oxidation of 3-CCA[65]. To further evaluate the overall ROS generation capability in the resultant samples, we analyzed the fluorescence intensity, as shown in Figure S3. The L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample exhibited a higher fluorescence intensity compared to the Ag2S and Ag counterparts in the presence of DCFH-DA under UVVisibleNIR light exposure. This increased in intensity indicates that the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample produced a greater amount of ROS, demonstrating its enhanced ROS generation capability. Moreover, no ROS formation was detected in the absence of light irradiation, further ensuring the light-dependent nature of the ROS generation process. Furthermore, the ESR results depicted in Figure S4(a, b) provide additional confirmation of the •O2 – and OH• radicals generation in all three samples under 15 min of NIR laser irradiation. In the absence of light (Dark), no detectable radical signals were observed, again emphasizing the dependence of ROS generation on light activation. Relative scrutiny revealed that the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs produce a higher ratio of •O2 – and OH• radicals, suggesting their efficient photodynamic degradation potential against MDR pathogenic bacteria. The enhanced radical formation compared to Ag and Ag2S NPs under NIR-laser irradiation highlights an accelerated photocatalytic reaction rate, thereby indicating the promising potential of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs for advanced photodynamic antibacterial applications.

To evaluate the photothermal properties under NIR-laser illumination, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs dispersion exhibits a broad absorption band exceeding beyond 808 nm, as depicted in Fig. 4a. This indicates the potential of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs for efficient photothermal conversion under NIR-laser illumination. It can also be seen that the absorption intensity is thoroughly increased with the increase of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs concentration. To further assess the photothermal effect, the temperature change of the NPs under 808 nm of NIR-light irradiation at 2 W cm−2 power density and at varying concentrations was continuously recorded, as shown in Fig. 4b. In general, the blank sample (ultrapure water) shows no significant change in the temperature under 808 nm NIR-light illumination. Conversely, the NPs samples demonstrate a temperature increase over irradiation duration with the temperature rise being positively correlated with the NPs concentration. It is worth mentioning that the temperature increased to 79.4 ℃ after 8 min of irradiation at 60 μg mL−1 concentration, which is quite high to efficiently kill pathogenic strains. These outcomes demonstrate the excellent photothermal conversion ability of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs under 808 nm NIR-laser irradiation, which can be attributed to the non-radiative relaxation of exciton in the conduction band of the respective material[66]. The corresponding infrared (IR) thermographic images (Fig. 4c) of the NPs dispersion with different concentrations and irradiation durations indicate high photothermal effects with concentrations controlled adjustable temperatures. As the concentrations and irradiation time increased, the counterplots became more radiative and homogenous, indicating a rise in temperature and more uniform heat dissipation. The corresponding temperature uniformityTU was calculated to be approximately 38.72 % using TU (%) = (TH ─ TL)/2 T(avg) x 100 relation[67]. Where, the subscript H, L, and avg denote the respective highest, lowest, and average detected temperature values. The uniform spatial distribution of temperature within the suspension during light exposure serves as a measure of NPs effective heating performance. The reversible photothermal stability of the 60 μg mL−1 NPs dispersion was evaluated after consecutive ten cycles of laser on/off treatment and the obtained result showed that the corresponding NPs maintained excellent photothermal stability (Fig. 4d). From the results (Figs. 4e and 4f), the photothermal conversion efficiencyη of the NPs was found to be 38.4 % (detailed calculations are shown in ESI) according to a previously reported method[54,55], which is higher than those of many reported photothermal materials, including PDA coated black phosphorous-BP (35.4 %)[68], PVA-coated BP (31.5 %)[69], CG/PDA@Ag (38.2 %)[70], and BSA@Ag2S (18.8 %)[71],. The increased phonon generation due to the nonradiative recombination at intrinsic defect sites of Ag2S-x NPs can lead to excellent conversion of light energy into heat, suggesting that the NPs heat up more efficiently under NIR-laser irradiation, ultimately enhancing their overall photothermal performance. The Raman spectrum of the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs in Figure S5 reveled defect related-peaks that are more pronounced than those in the Ag2S counterpart further supporting a strong correlation between higher defect density and increased phonon activity, which ultimately contributes to superior heat generation. Recently two-dimensional2D TMSs, such as MoS2, WS2, and TiS2 have been extensively used as PTA and PDA agents in the field of biomedicines[17,72,73], however, there are still some main challenges that need to be resolved in clinical applications. One of the primary challenges is developing a reliable synthesis technique to produce such 2D materials with controlled size, shape, purity, and surface charge[72]. On the other hand, the stability of the 2D nanomaterials presents another significant challenge. In contrast, the Ag2S-based hybrid-NPs developed in this study possess superior characteristics, such as excellent optical stability and NIR tunable emission, attributed to the facile coupling of S-Cdots, the formation of in-situ S-x-vacancies, and effective surface functionalization. These properties make them widely applicable in bioimaging, diagnostic, sensing, catalysis, and other biomedical fields applications. Accordingly, these results demonstrate that the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs efficiently convert light energy into heat, suggesting that the non-stoichiometric Ag2S NPs is an efficient photothermal agent for NIR-laser thermal ablation of MDR pathogens.

The antibacterial activity of the as-prepared NPs was examined against two different MDR pathogens (Gram-negative─E. coli-25922 and Gram-positive─MRSA-1369) using the microbiological plate counting technique, as illustrated in Fig. 5. Owing to their existing phototriggered biocidal competences, these Ag-based NPs demonstrate great potential in advancing antimicrobial research and innovations. To evaluate this hypothesis, we assessed the antibacterial susceptibility of the as-prepared NPs at different concentrations (0, 60, 90, and 120 μg mL−1) both in the dark and upon NIR-light irradiation, and compared the obtained results with the control groups of the experiment, as depicted in Fig. 5a-d. The comparative bactericidal results revealed that the L- PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs exhibit superior antibacterial activity against both bacterial strains compared to its Ag2S and Ag NPs counterparts, both in the absence and presence of NIR-light irradiation (Fig. 5a-b). It can be seen that, in the absence of hybrid-NPs (0 μg mL−1) treatment, both bacterial species reached their highest growth phase with or without NIR-light illumination, serving as the blank control group of the experiment. When the concentrations of the antibacterial suspension containing Ag, Ag2S, and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs were introduced, the growth of both bacterial strains steadily declined both in the dark and upon NIR-laser treatment conditions. More precisely, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs sample showed robust effectiveness against both bacterial species even at the lowest concentration of 60 μg mL−1 under NIR-light illumination in contrast to its Ag2S (90 μg mL−1) and Ag (120 μg mL−1) counterparts, respectively. Ciprofloxacin was used as a positive control at the concentration of 30 μg mL−1, demonstrating the lowest bacterial survival percentage. In contrast, the NIR-laser irradiated L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPstreated E. coli and MRSA samples (at a dose of 60 μg mL−1) showed a comparable effectiveness to the standard ciprofloxacin antibiotic. The dose-dependent antibacterial activity of the resultant hybrid-NPs was investigated at varying (0, 15, 30, 45, and 60 μg mL−1) gradient concentrations against both bacterial strains, as illustrated in Figure S6a-c (see the ESI for details). Furthermore, the bar diagrams in Fig. 5c-d quantify these observed bactericidal results, illustrating the percentage rate of bacterial survival. The data reveal that the bacterial strains treated with and without NIR-laser in the absence of antibacterial NPs (blank group) exhibited higher bacterial growth proportion. Conversely, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs-treated E. coli and MRSA samples with and/or without NIR-laser irradiation exhibited the lowest bacterial survival proliferation in contrast to those treated with Ag2S, Ag NPs, or bare L-PEI counterparts (Figure S7), and paralleled the effectiveness of the standard ciprofloxacin antibiotics in the case of NIR-laser treated samples. In addition, these outcomes were further confirmed by the bacterial growth curve assay, in which both E. coli and MRSA treated with L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs under NIR-laser exhibited no bacterial growth proliferation in culture, whereas the untreated bacteria (control group) entered the logarithm phase of rapid growth after a short lag period and then achieved a stable phase after 10 h (Figure S8a, b). In brief, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample shows immense promise in inhibiting the growth of both pathogenic strains under NIR-laser irradiation, even at the lowest designated concentration of the MIC study. This effectiveness is likely stem from its multi-hit therapeutic potential, including its excellent interaction with the bacterial cell walls, controlled release of Ag+ with the piercing ability of the NPs, efficient generation of ROS radicals, and excellent photothermal conversion efficiency under a single wavelength of 808 nm NIR-laser, ultimately resulting in robust photo-triggered inactivation of pathogenic strains.

To further interpret the bactericidal performance of the developed NPs, we performed the SEM characterization of the bacterial samples to investigate the surface morphology of the bacterial cells, as depicted in Fig. 6a. The corresponding SEM images revealed wrinkled, aggregated, and damaged bacterial cell morphology when exposed to Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs under NIR-laser irradiation, whereas the untreated cells (blank control) retained their compact, intact, and typical structural shape under the same irradiation condition. The later result illustrates that the NIR-laser treatment in the absence of antibacterial NPs has no impact on bacterial cell viability. The photoinactivation of the respective bacterial strains can be explained by the absorption of NIR-light by these NPs, which leads to excess amounts of ROS generation and thermal heating effects under 808 nm of NIR-laser illumination. Additionally, the robust adsorption of the cationic Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs on the negatively charged bacterial cell surface via electrostatic adherence results in enhanced bacterial inactivation. Furthermore, the presence of small NPs on the bacterial surfaces can be attributed to the strong adsorption of the Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs facilitated by these electrostatic forces. The strong adsorption potential of the bactericidal NPs on the pathogenic cell surfaces is crucial to achieve efficient biocidal effects, particularly for materials with positively charged surfaces. To further confirm the destructive nature of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs on bacterial membranes under NIR-laser treatment, the integrity of the bacterial cell membrane was examined using confocal laser scanning microscopy, as depicted in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the live bacteria stained with 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindoleDAPI exhibited blue fluorescence, while the dead bacteria labeled with propidium iodidePI emitted red fluorescence. The blank control group displays blue fluorescence emission with almost no red spots of fluorescence indicating no biocidal activity in the absence of antibacterial NPs in both cases of E. coli and MRSA. In contrast, some red spots of fluorescence were observed for the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPstreated samples even in the absence of NIR-laser treatment indicating strong adsorption potential, which eventually resulted in multi-hit therapeutic efficacy and damage to bacterial cell membranes. Meanwhile, after being treated with L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs for 8 min of NIR-laser irradiation sample, the bacterial cells emitted more intense red fluorescence signals. This indicates that the resulting hybrid-NPs exhibit excellent NIR-light-induced photo-triggered destruction capability against both bacterial strains, which is consistent with the earlier antibacterial assay results. Consequently, these findings reveal that the strong attachment of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs to bacterial cells enhances their susceptibility to combined photodynamic and photothermal effects, ultimately resulting in remarkable photo-triggered bacterial cell lysis.

Based on the aforementioned experimental results, the eradication effect of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs sample on pathogenic biofilms was further investigated using the crystal violet staining approach, where the intensity of purple colorization correlates with the formation of bacterial biofilm. As shown in Fig. 7a-d, the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs exhibited negligible differences in biofilm eradication for both tested bacterial strains in the absence of NIR-laser irradiation, resulting in a minimum reduction in purple color intensity for 60 μg mL−1 NIR (-)treated samples. However, after treatment with L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs combined with NIR-laser irradiation, the purple colorization was visibly reduced, confirming that the synergistic photothermal and photodynamic effects of the hybrid-NPs effectively eradicated bacterial biofilms as illustrated in the bar graphs of Fig. 7c, d. This enhanced efficacy can be attributed to the combined heat generation and ROS formation under NIR-laser which compromise the structural integrity of the biofilm, overcoming the biofilm’s protective matrix and leading to a significant decrease in bacterial viability. Thus, the L-PEI coated and S-Cdots-modified Ag2S NPs with S-x-vacancies develop a multifunctional platform for combating biofilm-associated infection, particularly when coupled with NIR-laser-triggered therapy.

In consideration to their versatile antibacterial mechanisms, we further intend to explore the underlying photodynamic antibacterial mechanism of the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs. As an excellent semiconductor photocatalyst, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs produce elevated levels of ROS radicals under NIR-laser, which are responsible for the photoinactivation of targeted pathogens. The absorption of the NIR-light by L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs photocatalyst generates e-/h+ pairs, which then undergo a series of reactions to produce cytotoxic ROS including •O2 – and •OH radicals through
O
2
→
O
2
• −
↔
OH
•
reactions[23,34]. These highly reactive radicals cause oxidative damage to essential biomolecules of bacterial cells by first damaging their surfaces, which leads to leakage of the internal components and eventually results in bacterial cell death ( Fig. 8). Understanding the precise mechanism of bacterial growth suppression is important, and numerous detailed reports shed light on the cytotoxic effects of various nanomaterials on drug-resistant pathogens[24,74,75]. The principal mechanism for combating bacterial growth involves the direct interaction of the nanomaterials with bacterial cell walls, resulting in physical disruption and subsequent leakage of cellular contents, ultimately suppressing pathogenic growth. In such a case, materials size with their specific surface area play crucial roles in influencing their bactericidal effectiveness[26,65]. Another bacterial mechanism involves the leaching of metal ions in the pathogenic culture medium, which exert oxidative and/or enzymatic stress and subsequently permeate bacterial cell membranes that hindering cellular function and eventually resulting in bacterial membrane disruption. Also, in this case, the controlled release of metal ions, the particle size/shape, and the specific microorganism type are important[17,26]. The third key mechanism involves the generation of cytotoxic ROS radicals when exposed to light, which make a direct interaction with the bacterial cellular components. This interaction induces intense oxidative damage to the bacterial antioxidant system, which collectively disrupts lipid metabolism, cell proteins, and DNA, finally resulting in the inhibition of pathogenic growth and proliferation[18,32]. In our case, the photocatalytic mechanism of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs under NIR-laser was significantly enhanced due to the combined effects of the L-PEI coating and the generation of S-x-vacancies with in Ag2S matrix, through the incorporation of S-Cdots, which together improve the charge carrier separation and absorption efficiency. We applied Muliken’s electronegativity theory to compute the reduction potential of the conduction bandCB and valence bandVB, which provides valuable insights into the electronic properties of the materials[33].

(6) ECB = EVB – Eg

Here EC stands for the scaling factor 4.50 eV that bridges the absolute vacuum scale with the normal hydrogen electrodeNHE scale, X is the average electronegativity of the constituent atoms in the semiconductor materials, which is nearly 5.01 eV for Ag2S, and Eg, EVB, ECB represent bandgap energy, valence and conduction bands, respectively. The calculated edge potentials for CB and VB are –0.67 V and 1.71 V, respectively, suggesting that the redox potential of the CB-electrons is sufficiently negative compared to the standard redox potentials of oxygen in aqueous solution (E° O2/•O2 – = –0.34 V vs. NHE, E° O2/•HO2 = –0.14 V vs. NHE)[23,34], aligning well with its band structure. Based on these calculations, the energy band position of the non-stoichiometric Ag2S (Eg = 2.63 eV, ECB = −0.67 eV, and EVB = 1.71 eV) and the presence of excess amines group in the L-PEI together with S-Cdots are conducive for the effective separation and conveyance of the PCCs upon NIR-laser irradiation. This makes it easier for the surface-adsorbed oxygen molecules to be photochemically converted into cytotoxic ROS, as illustrated in the schematic diagram of the antibacterial mechanism (Fig. 8). Combined with the ESR results, it is coherent to believe that the CBelectrons might play a role in promoting the generation of the highly reactive •O2 – and •OH radicals through reduction pathways outlined in Eq. 7 10. This process is facilitated by the presence of S-x-vacancies in the Ag2S matrix, which enhance electron mobility and contribute to defect-mediated ROS production. These vacancies act as trapping centers for electrons, which are subsequently released into the CB and transferred to the S-Cdots, ultimately resulting in heightened photo reactivity and more efficient active radical formation through enhanced electrons transfer. Nonetheless, the photoexcited VB-holes are unable to directly oxidize water molecules to generate •OH radicals through a direct oxidation pathway (Eq. 11), as the VB potential of the Ag2S is lower than the standard redox potential of H2O/•OH (2.38 eV vs. NHE)[76]. Therefore, the photoexcited h+ can serve as a direct active species for bactericidal action. Additionally, the L-PEI effectively captures the VB-holes due to the abundance of amine groups, facilitating the separation of PCCs and promoting the generation of ROS—•O2 –, •OH radicals, and h+. These outcomes are well consistent with the obtained results of both active species trapping assay and ESR analysis, confirming the production of •O2 –, •OH, and h+ in the resultant L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs system. In addition, it is noteworthy that, aerobic microbes inherently produced heightened levels of free radicals as a consequence of their intracellular activities (Eqs. 12 and 13) [77,78]. In typical circumstances, a pathogenic cell encompasses endogenous substances that engage in a chemical reaction with such free radicals, resulting in the formation of stable molecules essential to maintaining the integrity of the respiratory chain of the mitochondria[34]. However, sever oxidative stress can be caused by continuous exposure to harmful ROS from external sources, which can distress this delicate equilibrium. The targeted bacteria's entire enzymatic system can be disrupted by the relentless influx of excessive ROS, which would ultimately compromise the microorganism's ability to function[55,67]. To sum up the above discussion, the as-synthesized L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs exhibit strong potential as a photocatalyst for antibacterial applications. The cationic nature of L-PEI facilitates electrostatic interaction with bacterial membranes (Eq. 14), promoting effective surface anchoring. Upon NIR-exposure, the system generates elevated levels of cytotoxic ROS—•O2 –, •OH, and h+ (Eq. 15), together with a controlled release of Ag+, as confirmed by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, see the ESI for details). These combined mechanisms induce highly oxidative stress, which disrupts the bacterial cell membrane and ultimately leads to the effective elimination of the pathogens.

The existence of intracellular ROS in the prepared hybrid-NPs was further confirmed by measuring the respective protein levels and catalase activity, as illustrated in Fig. 9. It can be seen in Fig. 9a that the protein level in both tested pathogenic strains was decreased in contrast to blank control, suggesting that the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample effectively inactivated the protein synthesis in both cases. In parallel, Fig. 9b illustrates an increase in the catalase activity of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs treated bacterial species compared to the untreated NPs sample, indicating excellent biocidal activity with increased amounts of ROS generation within the bacterial cell membrane. Notably, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs sample exhibited a comparable reduction in protein levels and a significant increase in catalase activity relative to the standard ciprofloxacin group, further validating its potent antibacterial mechanism. As a consequence, the corresponding variation in protein levels and catalase activity in both Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs-treated bacterial samples provide insight into the presence of active species, strongly in favor of the reactive intracellular ROS production in both cases.
