⟶ ⟶ Antibacterial ⁢
activity

The well-developed hybrid-NPs exhibit significant bactericidal activity, demonstrating great potential as antibacterial agents, which can directly interact with pathogenic strains, penetrate their cell membranes, and cause disruption and leakage to cellular components. Furthermore, their enhanced ROS generation potential induces high oxidative stress and eventually leads to corresponding cell death. Compared to previously reported antibacterial agents, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs in this study are particularly notable for their effectiveness at low dosages, as illustrated in Table S1. Despite significant research efforts, the exact mechanism by which nanomaterials exert their antibacterial effects remain incompletely elucidated and not fully understood in the literatures to date[34,79]. Alternatively, in-silico molecular docking studies have been extensively employed over the past few decades to explore the mechanisms underlying the biocidal activity of nanomaterials. These studies precisely investigate the interactions between the biomolecules and nano-agents, elucidate the antibacterial mechanisms of various drugs, and support the design of novel bioactive materials with exceptional biocidal activity. It is well-known that the enzymes are vital for bacterial growth, metabolism, and survival, and are therefore considered prime targets for the development of new antimicrobial drugs and their derivatives[32,80].

Previously, it has been indicated that β-lactamase and DNA-gyrase are promising targets for the development of novel antibiotics, potentially effective against MDR pathogenic strains[81]. In this work, we investigated the binding affinity of the Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S- Cdots NPs to the active sites of the β-lactamase and DNA-gyrase obtained from the E. coli and MRSA strains, respectively. A 3D structural depiction of the NPs DNA-gyrase
MRSA superimposed complex (Figure S10a, b) is provided to elucidate the NPs interaction with the active pockets of the DNA-gyrase enzyme. The optimal docking conformation of the Ag2S NPs sample with the active pockets of the β-lactamase
E. coli enzyme showed H-bonding interactions with Glu 245 (2.4 Å), Pro181 (2.2 Å), Asn244 (2.0 Å, 2.8 Å), Asp242 (2.6 Å, 2.9 Å), and Arg232 (2.5 Å), yielding a total binding score of −17.385 Kcal mol−1. Similarly, the top-ranked docking conformation of the Ag2S with DNA-gyrase
MRSA displayed H-bond interactions with Thr165 (1.0, and 2.3 Å), Asp73 (1.4 Å, and 2.2 Å), and Gly77 (1.0, 1.6 A), along with C-H bond interactions with Ans46 (2.3 A), Glu50 (0.9 A), and Ile78 (1.6), resulting in a total binding score of −11.482 Kcal mol−1. The corresponding binding interactions between Ag2S and both β-lactamase
E. coli and DNA-gyrase
MRSA enzymes is depicted in Figure S11a, b. In contrast, the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs sample demonstrated H-bond interactions with the active pockets of the β-lactamase
E. coli protein, such as Glu 245 (1.2 Å), Pro181 (1.4 Å, 2.0 Å), Asn244 (1.6 Å, 2.1 Å), Asp242 (2.3 Å, 1.8 Å), and Arg232 (2.3 A), along with C-H bond interactions with Ile243 (2.0 A, 2.3 Å), and Glu228 (2.3 Å), achieving an overall binding score of −24.652 Kcal mol−1. Similarly, the top-ranked docking conformation of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs with DNA-gyrase
MRSA enzyme exhibited similar H, and C-H bond interactions (Thr165 (0.9, and 1.7 Å), Asp73 (1.3 Å, and 0.5 Å), and Gly77 (1.0, 1.3 Å), Ans46 (1.4 Å and 1.6 Å), Glu50 (0.7 Å), and Ile78 (0.6)), demonstrating a strong binding affinity with a total binding score of −19.385 Kcal mol−1. Fig. 10a, b illustrates the involvement of amino acid residues in H-bonds, including Asp73 (1.3 Å, and 0.5 Å), Gly77 (1.0, 1.3 Å), Thr165 (0.9, and 1.7 Å) interactions between L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs and both β-lactamase
E. coli and DNA-gyrase
MRSA enzymes. These binding interactions indicate that both Ag2S and L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs have the capability to target the β-lactamase and DNA-gyrase, which are crucial proteins involved in bacterial resistance mechanisms. The obtained results of in-silico molecular docking studies are strongly correlated with the developed hybrid-NPs in vitro biocidal activity, indicating their potential as a promising inhibitor of both DNA-gyrase
MRSA and β-lactamase
E. coli enzymes. These findings hold great promise in advancing the development of novel antimicrobial nano agents to tackle antimicrobial resistanceAMR in the healthcare sector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the hemocompatibility and cytocompatibility experiments were conducted to evaluate the biocompatibility of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs, which is crucial for their use in photoresponsive antibacterial applications. The in vitro hemocompatibility test revealed that the Triton-treated red blood cellsRBCs displayed bright red color supernatant, while the hybrid-NPs at different concentrations (30120 μg mL−1) and PBS-treated RBCs groups demonstrated transparent color of supernatant, as depicted in Fig. 11a. Further, Fig. 11b revealed that the values of optical densityOD at 545 nm for both PBS- and hybrid-NPs (at 30120 μg mL−1 concentrations) are significantly lower than those of the Triton-treated group. The respective hemolysis ratios of the hybrid-NPs at varying concentrations are below 5 %, indicating a safe level for hemostatic materials. These findings demonstrate the minimal hemolytic effect of the designed hybrid-NPs on RBCs, suggesting their suitability for safe applications as hemostatic agents in biomedical settings. Subsequently, the cytotoxicity of the hybrid-NPs to MC3T3-E1 cells was assessed using MTT test and fluorescence staining of the cells ( Fig. 12a, b). Fig. 12a depicts the percentage viability of the MC3T3-E1 cells after being treated with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 120 μg mL−1 of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs. Cell viability exceeded 90 % at the concentration of 60 μg mL−1, and remained above 80 % even at double (120 μg mL−1) concentration, demonstrating excellent biocompatibility of the L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs. In addition, the fluorescence staining of the cell in Fig. 12b illustrates distinct cell shapes, suggesting remarkable sample in-vitro biocompatibility. In short, these results indicate that the hybrid-NPs exhibit minimal hemolytic activity on RBCs and demonstrate low cytotoxicity towards MC3T3-E1 cells, confirming their biocompatibility for potential biomedical applications.

In summary, L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots hybrid-NPs exhibit exceptional antibacterial performance against both Gram-negative–E. coli and Gram-positive–MRSA bacterial strains, attributed to the synergistic effects of all components in the system, enabling direct interaction with bacterial cell membranes. This interaction leads to outer membrane disruption through high local heat and an elevated level of ROS production, as well as controlled release of Ag+ under a single wavelength of NIR-laser irradiation, ultimately resulting in multi-hit therapeutic potential for bacterial eradication. Importantly, the hybrid-NPs system demonstrates reduced cytotoxicity, enhancing its potential for safe biomedical applications. Therefore, we believe that these multifunctional antibacterial hybrid-NPs can be mass-produced using cost-effective synthesis and readily available raw materials to effectively eradicate a range of drug-resistant uropathogens, thereby reducing the risk allied with the overuse of traditional antibiotics.

In summary, we successfully developed NIR-responsive L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs through a facile one-step solvothermal synthesis technique, demonstrating strong photo-triggered antibacterial activity against both E. coli and MRSA uropathogenic strains. The formation of an internal electric field between nonstoichiometric Ag2S and embedded S-Cdots across the conductive L-PEI interface significantly enhanced photo-induced charge transport and ROS generation. ESR and Free-radical scavenging analyses confirm the generation of cytotoxic ROS •O2 –, •OH, while the NIR irradiation further amplified synergistic photodynamic and photothermal effects. Importantly, the hybrid-NPs achieved > 99 % bacterial growth inhibition under 8 min of NIR-light irradiation at a low MIC of 60 μg mL−1, which is comparable to standard antibioticciprofloxacin. The improved bactericidal efficacy is attributed to the L-PEI coating, which strengthens electrostatic interactions and promotes stable anchoring of Ag2S-x/S-Cdots NPs onto the bacterial surfaces. This interaction triggered outer membrane disruption through localized heating, elevated level of ROS generation, and controlled release of Ag+ under a single wavelength (808 nm) of NIR-laser irradiation, thus resulting in multifunctional cytotoxicity and damage to bacterial cells. The in-silico molecular docking results supported these in vitro findings by revealing a strong binding affinity against β-lactamase
E. coli and DNA-gyrase
MRSA enzymes, highlighting the potential of L-PEI@Ag2S-x/S-Cdots as photo-triggered antibacterial agents and promising alternatives to conventional antibiotics.
